post0.title: From GAN to WGAN
post0.content: In original version of GAN, the measure of real and generated examples was formulated as binary clasification: is it real or is it generated. Let's look at the simple case, where we have true distribution (in other words real examples) that consists of 0's. A perfect discriminator will return 1 when input to discriminator is 0 and 0 otherwise. Despite the fact that discriminator is doing great job at discriminating between real and generated, however, generator does not receive meaningful direction for improving. For example, generator output of 0.1 and 1 will be scored the same, even though 0.1 is much closer to real distribution. Let's see this in action. . import tensorflow as tf import numpy as np from matplotlib import pyplot as plt np.set_printoptions(suppress=False, precision=5) . Discriminator . def d_loss(d_score_z, d_score_r): batch_size = d_score_z.shape[0] d_loss_z =tf.keras.losses.binary_crossentropy(tf.zeros(shape = [batch_size,1], dtype=tf.float32), d_score_z) d_loss_r =tf.keras.losses.binary_crossentropy(tf.ones(shape = [batch_size,1], dtype=tf.float32), d_score_r) return tf.reduce_mean(d_loss_z + d_loss_r) def define_discriminator(act="sigmoid"): d_optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=0.05) # Discriminator discriminator = tf.keras.models.Sequential() discriminator.add(tf.keras.layers.Dense(32, activation="relu")) discriminator.add(tf.keras.layers.Dense(32, activation="relu")) discriminator.add(tf.keras.layers.Dense(1, activation=act)) return discriminator, d_optimizer . def train_discriminator(discriminator, d_optimizer, d_loss, batch_size = 64): real_data = tf.zeros([batch_size, 1]) # Train discriminator till perfection for i in range(1000): with tf.GradientTape() as tape: z = tf.random.normal(shape=[batch_size, 1]) d_score_z, d_score_r = discriminator(z), discriminator(real_data) total_loss = d_loss(d_score_z, d_score_r) if i % 100 == 0: print("Total loss:", total_loss.numpy(), "Scores for real:", d_score_r.numpy().mean(), "Scores for generated:", d_score_z.numpy().mean()) grads = tape.gradient(total_loss, discriminator.trainable_variables) d_optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, discriminator.trainable_variables)) . Disclaimer: we are creating artifial situation just to demonstrate underlying issue with using binary classification as loss for discriminator. In reality, we would not train discriminator in this fashion. . discriminator, d_optimizer = define_discriminator() train_discriminator(discriminator, d_optimizer, d_loss) . Total loss: 1.4474053 Scores for real: 0.5 Scores for generated: 0.5287869 Total loss: 0.009800203 Scores for real: 0.9927625 Scores for generated: 0.0023413328 Total loss: 0.008918599 Scores for real: 0.9911209 Scores for generated: 1.6848082e-09 Total loss: 0.0043402864 Scores for real: 0.99655956 Scores for generated: 0.0008768295 Total loss: 0.04669003 Scores for real: 0.9937058 Scores for generated: 0.014445845 Total loss: 0.0074086864 Scores for real: 0.9927226 Scores for generated: 0.00010457821 Total loss: 0.0013395697 Scores for real: 0.99866116 Scores for generated: 1.4081239e-08 Total loss: 0.059076376 Scores for real: 0.99894047 Scores for generated: 0.015243756 Total loss: 0.0085791545 Scores for real: 0.99145746 Scores for generated: 4.2778964e-08 Total loss: 0.014346502 Scores for real: 0.98575586 Scores for generated: 1.4063318e-09 . So we have got nearly perfect discriminator that gives 1 for real distribution and 0 for anything else. Let's train generator using such discriminator. . Generator . def g_loss(x, discriminator=discriminator): return tf.reduce_mean(1.0-discriminator(x)) def train_generator(g_loss, batch_size=64): g_optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=0.05) generator = tf.keras.layers.Dense(1, activation='tanh') for i in range(1000): with tf.GradientTape() as tape: z = tf.random.normal(shape=[batch_size, 1]) gen = generator(z) loss = g_loss(gen) if i % 100 == 0: print("Loss:", loss.numpy(), "Generator output: ",tf.reduce_mean(tf.abs(gen)).numpy()) grads = tape.gradient(loss, generator.trainable_variables) g_optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, generator.trainable_variables)) t = plt.scatter(np.zeros(z.shape), z.numpy(), label="True") g = plt.scatter(gen.numpy(), z.numpy(), alpha=0.5) plt.xlim(-1, 1) plt.legend((t,g),("True distribution", "Generated distribution")) . train_generator(g_loss) . Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.40880466 Loss: 0.9999944 Generator output: 0.58021176 Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.6349109 Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.6429486 Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.7314222 Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.73145306 Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.7356732 Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.7346891 Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.73358834 Loss: 1.0 Generator output: 0.7349014 . It does not converge, the generator does not learn to produce 0's and loss does not decrease. Even if we manage to find a way to train so that it converges, ideally we would want something that would be able to converge in this situation as well. . This issue was described in famous Wasserstein GAN paper. In this paper authors proposed to change binary clasification to something that measures distance between generated outputs and real examples. . Let's try it out with our simplified example. . WGAN . A very naive approach would be to measure Euclidean distance between real and generated distributions. Lets see if that helps. . def euclidean_distance(x): return tf.reduce_mean(tf.norm(x, 'euclidean')) train_generator(euclidean_distance) . Loss: 6.327228 Generator output: 0.73449063 Loss: 0.11322648 Generator output: 0.0123071335 Loss: 0.06942807 Generator output: 0.007305301 Loss: 0.033794746 Generator output: 0.0035097324 Loss: 0.05336855 Generator output: 0.0062940647 Loss: 0.05685883 Generator output: 0.00582584 Loss: 0.075475305 Generator output: 0.0073759733 Loss: 0.070027 Generator output: 0.006990136 Loss: 0.04942812 Generator output: 0.004983089 Loss: 0.020415433 Generator output: 0.0019976243 . It works in this example, we can see that loss is decresing and generator output approaches 0. However, this works in this specific scenario, we can use euclidean distance when we measure distance between 1D points, but how about images? Hence, for general solution we will need something that works universally. And that is what Wasserstein GAN paper is about. . For the sake of this post, we will skip the details of how and why WGAN works (there are a few brilliant posts about that, please checkout out links in "Further reading" section below), but to put it simply, we can use the distance between the outputs of the discriminator as proxy for distances between distributions. However, there is one important condition: we have to enforce a Lipschitz constraint. We will ignore Lipschitz constraint for now. . Let's try it out. . We measure the distance between the outputs of discriminator by simply distracting . def d_loss_wgan(d_score_z, d_score_r): return tf.reduce_mean(d_score_z - d_score_r) . discriminator_wgan, d_optimizer = define_discriminator(act=None) train_discriminator(discriminator_wgan, d_optimizer, d_loss_wgan) . Total loss: -0.20631 Scores for real: 0.0 Scores for generated: -0.20631 Total loss: -92241.375 Scores for real: -128.61824 Scores for generated: -92370.0 Total loss: -1122561.8 Scores for real: -766.5613 Scores for generated: -1123328.2 Total loss: -4381084.5 Scores for real: -1648.9462 Scores for generated: -4382733.5 Total loss: -11101716.0 Scores for real: -2930.0134 Scores for generated: -11104646.0 Total loss: -20111968.0 Scores for real: -2881.5947 Scores for generated: -20114848.0 Total loss: -37373372.0 Scores for real: -2529.8655 Scores for generated: -37375904.0 Total loss: -62526256.0 Scores for real: -10314.391 Scores for generated: -62536570.0 Total loss: -69934790.0 Scores for real: -9289.29 Scores for generated: -69944080.0 Total loss: -135784350.0 Scores for real: -2764.6501 Scores for generated: -135787120.0 . Looks like it is working, loss is decreasing, although it is quite unusual to see negative loss . So now we have discriminator which gives high scores for real examples. So the objective for generator would be to get as high scores as possible, hence we need just -discriminator(generated) . def g_loss_wgan(x): return tf.reduce_mean(-discriminator_wgan(x)) train_generator(g_loss_wgan) . Loss: 86786170.0 Generator output: 0.3962338 Loss: 2060925.2 Generator output: 0.009125391 Loss: 2406305.5 Generator output: 0.011163255 Loss: 816975.6 Generator output: 0.0036342242 Loss: 1002175.4 Generator output: 0.004520608 Loss: 1894261.4 Generator output: 0.008713374 Loss: 2398592.2 Generator output: 0.011115221 Loss: 1497964.8 Generator output: 0.007058435 Loss: 2221005.2 Generator output: 0.010142955 Loss: 2540864.5 Generator output: 0.011403117 . Code above actually is in line with WGAN except as I mentioned before we are mising a method to enforce Lipschitz constraint. Three main changes are: . we removed sigmoid from last layer of discriminator | we change loss of discriminator to be the distance between real and generated outputs of discriminator | we change generator loss to be just opposite to discriminator loss for generated input. | . We can see that this algorithm converges and generated distribution approaches 0. This is all nice and good, however, as you can see, discriminator loss is actually a negative number, hence practitioners more often uses some sort of not saturating loss. For example, hinge loss which is defined as follows: . def d_loss_hinge(d_score_z, d_score_r): d_loss_real = tf.nn.relu(1.0 - d_score_r) # Note: this changed d_loss_fake = tf.nn.relu(1.0 + d_score_z) # Note: this changed return tf.reduce_mean(d_loss_real) + tf.reduce_mean(d_loss_fake) . discriminator_hinge, d_optimizer = define_discriminator(act=None) train_discriminator(discriminator_hinge, d_optimizer, d_loss_hinge) . Total loss: 2.136686 Scores for real: 0.0 Scores for generated: 0.13668607 Total loss: 0.093093544 Scores for real: 3.111452 Scores for generated: -67.01461 Total loss: 0.15125507 Scores for real: 2.5489194 Scores for generated: -136.51453 Total loss: 0.14914158 Scores for real: 2.294151 Scores for generated: -104.925865 Total loss: 0.05613767 Scores for real: 1.9415648 Scores for generated: -164.79243 Total loss: 0.10247024 Scores for real: 2.2024882 Scores for generated: -191.51102 Total loss: 0.05842448 Scores for real: 1.6237375 Scores for generated: -260.61096 Total loss: 0.062497437 Scores for real: 1.4973229 Scores for generated: -287.98523 Total loss: 0.035296395 Scores for real: 1.658842 Scores for generated: -315.43768 Total loss: 0.027891118 Scores for real: 2.0688531 Scores for generated: -338.8837 . The same non saturaing principle can be applied to generator . def g_loss_hinge(x): return tf.reduce_mean(tf.nn.relu(1.0-discriminator_hinge(x))) . train_generator(g_loss_hinge) . Loss: 367.35083 Generator output: 0.5992545 Loss: 0.0 Generator output: 0.00071904564 Loss: 0.013932567 Generator output: 0.004125871 Loss: 0.0029984536 Generator output: 0.0024912397 Loss: 0.0031498875 Generator output: 0.0035641724 Loss: 0.58684456 Generator output: 0.0054641888 Loss: 0.24629313 Generator output: 0.0034913966 Loss: 0.17224233 Generator output: 0.003717575 Loss: 0.0 Generator output: 0.0027503031 Loss: 0.0 Generator output: 0.0008251491 . That looks good, we are nearly done, but as I mentioned, we skipped "Lipschitz constraint" piece from Wasserstein GAN paper. We will focus on this in the next post. . Further Reading . Read-through: Wasserstein GAN | From GAN to WGAN | GAN — Wasserstein GAN & WGAN-GP | .

page1.title: About Me
page1.content: I am CTO co-founder of Biomatter Designs. Currently working on developing deep learning based tools for various aspects of protein engineering. .
